title: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan
legal_principles: Supreme Court's power to issue guidelines in absence of legislation
relevant_acts: Prevention of Sexual Harassment at Workplace Act 2013 (later enacted)
summary: Filed following gang rape of social activist Bhanwari Devi. The Supreme Court filled a legislative vacuum by issuing the 'Vishaka Guidelines' — binding directions on employers to prevent and address sexual harassment at the workplace. The guidelines remained in force until POSH Act 2013 was enacted.
key_holdings: Sexual harassment at workplace violates Articles 14, 15, 19(1)(g), and 21
Court can issue binding guidelines to fill legislative vacuum
Employers have a duty to prevent sexual harassment
Internal Complaints Committees to be established
International obligations (CEDAW) can inform domestic law
key_quotes: Gender equality includes protection from sexual harassment.
Where legislation is absent, the Supreme Court can issue binding guidelines.
why_important: Landmark judgment creating sexual harassment framework at workplace; directly led to enactment of POSH Act 2013. Established that courts can fill legislative gaps on human rights issues.
relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Relevant to HR compliance obligations for companies — POSH compliance is a legal mandate for all organisations.
